sentences.text: It was in the-- [SIGHS] in the third quarter, I guess, of '43.
A big-- a big transport came from Germany-- Hamburg, something like that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with American passports.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had some kind of visas, American passports.
And they took him in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They went to the crematorium, too.
Everything, you know, was distinguished with, with, with, with, with the signs and the washrooms and the, the, the numbers.
Remember where you put the drinks and put away the things.
And then after that, you get the soup prepared.
And, and, and this is because of-- the Swiss agency demands this from the Americans.
You know, the Swiss-- they have to go through Swiss.
So this is the last thing.
Anyway, when they brought in one transport, then they put him in, in, in the-- in the shower room there, and they held them for the next transport.
And they got wise, and they got-- and they got nervous.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they saw-- they saw what, what, what was going-- what happened.
So this woman [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] started to make a show there.
Inside there were some SS people.
She started dancing for them.
They looked at her excited.
All of a sudden, she, she, she, she grabbed the gun and she shoot him, Schillinger, oberscharfuhrer.
Schillinger-- Oberscharfuhrer Schillinger.
At that time, there was a, a big-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a big thing going on.
They didn't know how to handle it.
People came in, fresh, fresh people.
Inside there was shooting, so they started shooting uncontrollably, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And at the end of it, they killed all-- everybody.
They killed the whole, whole transport.
They killed the-- this was-- this was in, in, in, in '43, but the end of '43.
Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it came one time where a transport-- there was this prisoner from the sondercommando, which told a guy, you should know that you're going to be gassed.
This-- this is a woman.
She, she was hysterical.
She was hysterical and she started talking about she-- there were signs that they're going to the bath.
And she started talking about it to other people.
But meanwhile, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they didn't buy it.
It can't be, can't be.
So-- but as the time went on, they-- they didn't undress so fast.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they started pushing with dogs, dogs.
They saw-- they saw already that there's no other way.
They forced them.
By the end of it, they told this lady to-- she could stay out.
She, she told the story, stay out.
When they finished the rest of it there, they dialed up sondercommando, and she gave him out, this guy.
They burned him alive.
He was in the town from Wolbrom.
Wolbrom.
And the scharfuhrer was [POLISH].
[POLISH].
[POLISH].
First of all, he hanged him by the feet by the oven.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it-- and burned him halfway through the mouth.
Then they-- he told him to open the door, run-- he should run.
He run to the-- to the-- what do you call it?
Electric wires, and they kill him.
Burned him alive [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Coming to 1944, there was a selection to be made.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at that time-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --'44-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --'44 started Hungary, coming in from Hungary big, big transports from Hungary.
It was day and night with no end.
And at that time, there was Oberscharfuhrer [INAUDIBLE].
He took over from Foss.
Foss wasn't good enough.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So he, he was-- he was the guy who liquidated the first commander in 1942, which I told you they, they, they-- they, they, they, finished one commander in '42 before I came there.
This was the same one.
He was-- he was-- he was-- he was-- there's no words to, to, to explain what he was.
So as he had the experience from bunkers.
As the transports came in so heavy, I was in Crematorium 5.
And outside, there was maybe a few hundred feet-- a hundred, 200 feet-- where the electric things-- the, the, the guards were there so they dig their-- dig bunkers.
Beside the ovens, they dig the bunkers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he oversaw that, how to build it, and he gave the plans and, oh, it should run down from one side to the other.
And then if, if, if they-- if they burned just the corpses, then they took the fat and, and, and, and put-- and, and fired on top of the-- of the-- of the-- of the bodies in order to burn better.
And then he established also a, a big-- a bit platform where they took out the, the, the, the bones and they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] turned into dust, you know?
And they brought it to, to the-- to the river, to the Vistula there, the Vistula
near Auschwitz.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] at that time [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] it came-- it came in the-- in the-- in the-- trans-- in the hundreds of thousands.
And all the crematories were full.
It, it-- they could-- you couldn't-- you couldn't handle it as much as, as, as, as they came in.
This was-- at that time was Eichmann.
Eichmann was there in, in, in, in Hungary.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
What happened then?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once he catched, he he sorted out the guys from the commander in front of the watch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] See also, his was-- his, his, his, his system was to hang him by the-- by the feet.
You're talking about Foss?
Hm?
Moll. ?]
Moll. ?]
Moll, ?] not Foss.
Moll. ?] [?
Moll took ?] over.
And this, this was going on till the-- till the-- that'd be the end of '44.
In the middle-- no, in the middle of '44-- middle of '44.
How?
It was a few selections.
One, the couple had to say-- most of the couple-- in Auschwitz, with the system, more or less the older-- the older had the preference, the older numbers.
The ones which they hired later came the younger numbers.
Last in, first out.
That's how it was.
Then the couple already-- I was with him a long time so he spared me.
My cousin was there and from my-- from my number was there.
I mean, it was-- it wasn't so long, but it was long.
It was two selections which they took out.
They, they put in people-- as the transports came more, they took out people as they come-- came down.
So it, it-- they-- it was an inside selection.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] when the Hungarians was-- came in, they was-- they was-- they was busy from all the sides.

All the crematoriums and all, all the, the, the, the-- they built new bunkers and then they-- then they-- and before that, before the Hungrians, they took and a few hundred [INAUDIBLE], fresh ones, maybe 400 on top of this.
Then came a selection.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the selection-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'll go back a little bit.
The Kaminski was, was, was kapo on Crematorium 2.
At that time, I, I, I didn't finish what I-- what I-- what I finished there.
We couldn't convince the camp to do anything, to do it, because they, they put it off.
The time was playing for them, because the Russians came nearer.
Lublin was already-- at that time was already in the hands of, of, of Norway or, or, or they [? coming in ?].
The war turned around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how do I want to say-- sonderkommando went on their own.
I don't know.
I-- to tell you the truth, I was not in this politics.
I, I don't-- I don't know.
I was not.
There, there, there are people who were.
I wasn't.
I don't know.
They didn't take me into, into, to those things.
But one day, comes a special shipment-- a, a special car.
And they took us, the whole kommando of Crematorium 5, and put this in the-- in the-- in the-- in the inside not to work.
They close us up, and they did their own work.
What they did is they took out Kaminski from, from, from Crematorium 1-- 2, and they burned him.
They had something, some smell, that something was going on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they watched us.
This was '44.
The middle of '44.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '44.
Kaminski, he was a good guy.
He was the one who hired all the [POLISH], you know, guy who cut the hair.
He was-- he was really a-- but he, he, he had connections.
He had connections.
He pulled-- he pulled the, the strings.
But, but there-- I told him in the beginning there was one Mietek Morawa in Crematorium 1 in, in, in Auschwitz.
He killed us.
He was a son of a gun.
I don't want to express myself.
And he had an SS man there who protected him, actually protect him-- protect a big one, Weber, from, from, from [INAUDIBLE], from Auschwitz.
He gave him the bicycles to clean.
And then that's the way, because he, he, he, he, he was a, a-- what do you say, a [POLISH].
You know what I mean?
So they, they protected him and the oberkommando-- the over-- what's his name, [POLISH] or from the-- from the-- from the Gestapo, they put it-- they put it-- him into crematorium.
But later, when the crematorium was almost obsolete, when the big crematoriums were there, they took out all those-- the, the, the first commander which we came to them and they send him to us.
They sent us also Mietek Morawa, and there was one [INAUDIBLE], he was one [INAUDIBLE], the foreman.
Those three, they shipped them together to us.
In other words, whoever smelled the crematorium, they couldn't go out.
This is-- this is the end.
This is the end of today.
The end.
This is not-- [INAUDIBLE] no, this is end, end, end, end street.
So they said that he gave him out.
He gave out Kaminski because--
Mietek.
Somehow he wanted to smell them out or he would want-- would like to go.
And he gave him out.
That's-- so they, they, they, they, they kept him and brought him to our crematorium.
Also there was one was-- the name was, was [POLISH]-- [POLISH].
He-- when I-- when I was joining the kommando, I heard that he was before also in the kommando.
But somehow, he was-- he was taken back to Auschwitz and they, they interrogated him.
He was-- he, he was political.
He was political, and then he was against the Germans.
He-- and then they brought him back and made him kapo.
And then he was with, with my commander.
Together with Leizer, it was also he.
And once [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Crematorium 5 was built after the 2 and 3, so when they built the Crematoriums 4 and 5, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything was ready but electric was not ready.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So there was in the-- in the cook's place, there was a little window where the truck came to unload the-- it was a full-- a full-- a full room of cooks.
So once, it was in the evening, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had something to hide, food which I, I had.
So I put it in the cooks.
So this, this SS [POLISH]-- he wasn't an officer, a [INAUDIBLE] he was on the truck and his name was Kulsis.
He came in, he saw me there on the cooks.
He grabbed me and-- to throw me out.
And I know if he tells me to throw me out in the crematorium, that's the only way-- that's the only way to, to-- inside he can-- he can't do anything to me.
So he pushed me.
So-- and I didn't want to go.
So Wladek was there, and he saved me.
He saved me.
Somehow, word came to the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And shortly after came a, a, a truck.
Usually, they came from Silesia, came their trucks with, with, with, with buddies.
If somebody had, had a butcher or he sold meat or whatever, you know.
Whatever.
And they did it themself, the SS.
They put the body into the oven and the head was separate.
And then when they left, we recognized this was Wladek.
Because he took my part-- my, my, my, my side.
What?
[SIGHS] Sabotage what, the crematorium?
When you come to it, this was-- actually, they spoke about a big selection.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [BACKROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they assembled the people from Crematorium 4, Crematorium 5.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- one, one was against the other.
We were on 5 and on 4 was-- the lunch came on 4, the kettles.
And we went-- [INAUDIBLE] on 4, it was sad.
The, the people which have to be selected, a few people-- 200, 300 people had to be selected-- for, for, for transport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a revolt.
There was a revolt and with bare hands, knives, whatever this-- with SS.
At that time, everybody was running like-- we couldn't overcome them.
But they tried.
And I saw the SS, the-- our guards taken to bicycles and, and, and to telephones, whatever there was.
When I saw that, I told myself, there is no place to be here.
So since somebody was running, they threw grenades and hand-- hand.
So I said to myself, across the street is the crematorium.
I'm going to run to Crematorium 5.
So I run over the, the road and within seconds came, came this-- the SS came with trucks and controlled everything.
And, and [BACKROUND NOISE] I asked-- they-- the Crematorium 4 burnt the crematorium.
Who burnt the crematorium?
The, the prisoners burned the crematorium.
No, no, no.
They didn't get grenades.
I said the SS used grenades.
If somebody went in to hide themselves, they threw in grenades.
But in no time, there was-- there was-- there was a avalanche of SS.
And, and we had-- in Crematorium 5, there was a transport which we-- at work.
We just stopped for, for lunch and then come back.
When we came-- when, when, when the time came after lunch and we went back to work there was-- how many in there? 50 people.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They took the whole Crematorium 4, whoever was there-- whoever was there-- took him on the other side to us.
And there was this lawn.
Like I call it a lawn-- before you got into the crematorium, was a little lawn.
But the lawn was, was fenced around with, with, with little bushes you should-- you shouldn't be seen through.
And, and, and sometimes when transports came, if there wasn't place inside in the mortuary to undress-- because there-- there wasn't a mortuary in the undressing room-- it was-- in 2 and 3 it was.
So they told them to undress on, on this lawn.
So they took in the whole kommando and laid them out on the lawn.
They went from one to one and shoot them in the neck.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the whole night when this Crematorium 2 and 3 saw the fire from, from Crematorium 4, they start running off to the, the, the wires.
To make the story short, at that night we worked in Crematorium 5-- there was a transport-- and they brought in in the hundreds-- in the hundreds from, from, from those trying to escape.
And this was-- and this was happening exactly to the day it was [POLISH], [INAUDIBLE] 1944.
October, '44.
This was the [POLISH].
I was in 5 and I saw the fire there.
I saw there's no way-- there's only dead.
I saw there was only-- there's nothing else that can help me there, so I tried to run over the road.
Because 4 and 5 was like this side of the road and the other side of the road.
So it was one against the other.
So I run over.
It-- it, it, it-- [INAUDIBLE], you know what I mean?
It [INAUDIBLE].
Crematorium 5.
Crematorium 5, we had-- at that time, we were active in, in, in, in the-- in the crematorium.
So they left-- they let us stay.
They let us-- they let us stay.
Do the work.
Somebody has to do the work so they let us.
But later [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took us back to the block.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they started [POLISH] kommando.
[POLISH].
You know what means [POLISH]?
Destroy the, the, the crematoriums.
[POLISH].
Whoever was left [POLISH].
So we went to [POLISH] kommando.
And then sometime we stood on the block.
We didn't go to work.
All of a sudden came in the Schreiber.
He says he needs some, some people to go to work-- to work-- empty out the, the, the-- because on Crematorium 2, the commander when they would get work, they, they, they, they lived there.
They didn't take him out.
They didn't bring him to the work-- to the block back.
But he slept there.
The night shift slept and the day shift slept.
But at that-- at the time when the fire was there, there was a fire in, in Crematorium 2.
So they said we need workers to, to Crematorium-- empty out mattresses, whatever there was.
So I heard it.
I said, I don't want to stay here.
I don't want to stay in the block.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we cleaned out the, the, the-- all the burned things, whatever was.
We worked a few hours.
Going back, I found out-- I saw-- I saw my, my people going to the [POLISH], to the [POLISH].
You know what is the [POLISH].
My cousin and all, all the-- they went to the [POLISH] and all the old ones.
What happened is when, when, when I went to work, the lagerfuhrer-- before that it was the kapo or the blockalteste.
But at that time, it was the lagerfuhrer came in.
He took the charts.
He went instead of, of, of the back, he went to the front.
So he took all the old ones, you know what I mean?
So they called my name, my cousin's name.
He went.
And all those who came with my transport had to go.
They called my name.
I wasn't there.
They took the next one.
And I came back and I was-- I-- and, and, and this was the story what happened then.
A few people.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
From my kommando-- from the people-- when we came for lunch there, I saw there was no way-- there was no way-- they-- it, it, it-- waiting for the dead.
If they threw grenades, if somebody went to a hole, you took a grenade.
So I said, I don't want to be here.
I cannot win with them.
And, and, and they went on bicycles to call help.
And how long will it take?
This was-- this was the story of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with, with the-- with the-- with the what do you call it--
The [POLISH] commander, the [INAUDIBLE] commander.
[POLISH] was the was the lagerfuhrer.
[POLISH] from, from, from Birkenau.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This was-- this was the already end of the year.
End of the year-- end of the year was-- was-- this was after the [POLISH].
And we were-- went to clean up again to [POLISH].
All of a sudden, it was-- we heard in the camp that there was-- there is evacuation.
The next tape?
OK.
